brief_document.title: Workflow core: run, now, random, and the typed entry contract
brief_document.purpose: Define the heart of the author surface: workflow.run (the single recorded activity dispatch), the determinism primitives now/random bound to AD, and workflow.define (the typed entry contract the .aion manifest consumes). Establish that there is no side-effect escape hatch other than run.
brief_document.task: Create the aion/workflow module (declarations + re-exports) and the workflow/run.gleam and workflow/define.gleam submodules. Implement workflow.run as the single recorded activity dispatch via the ffi binding, encoding the input and decoding the typed output or a typed ActivityError. Bind workflow.now and workflow.random to AD's determinism context via ffi, with no wall-clock or entropy alternative. Define workflow.define for the typed entry contract.

requirements.title: workflow.run — the single recorded dispatch
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define workflow.run(Activity(i, o)) -> Result(o, ActivityError) as the single recorded activity dispatch, binding to the engine via the ffi layer, encoding the activity input and decoding the typed output, with no generic side-effect escape hatch.
acceptance: workflow/run.gleam defines run(Activity(i, o)) -> Result(o, ActivityError); it encodes the input via the activity's Codec, calls the ffi activity-dispatch binding (which records via AD and returns from history on replay), and decodes the result with the output Codec.
A decode failure on the returned payload surfaces as a typed error (the engine-originated decode variant from AF-003), never a panic.
No workflow.side_effect(fn) or equivalent is provided; a doc comment states that the only recorded side-effect path is run (and the concurrency combinators over activities) per CO7.
A harness-backed test (using AF-011's mock once available, or a stub binding) asserts run returns the decoded typed result on success and the typed ActivityError on failure.

requirements.title: Determinism: now and random
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define workflow.now() -> Timestamp and workflow.random() -> Float (plus random_int) bound to AD's determinism context via the ffi layer, exposing NO wall-clock or entropy-source binding.
acceptance: workflow.now() returns the recorded event timestamp from AD's determinism context (via the ffi binding), documented as the only time source available to workflow code.
workflow.random() -> Float and workflow.random_int(min, max) -> Int draw from AD's seeded RNG (keyed on WorkflowId + RunId), via the ffi binding.
The SDK exposes no binding to a wall clock (gleam stdlib / erlang:now) or entropy source; a doc comment states the prohibition on calling them from workflow code (CO6).
A test asserts now and random return values via the binding (using the harness's deterministic stub) and that repeated runs under the same seed produce the same random sequence.

requirements.title: workflow.define — typed entry contract
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define workflow.define(name, input_codec, output_codec, error_codec, entry_fn) returning an opaque WorkflowDefinition(input, output, error) value carrying the name, the three Codecs, and the entry function, which the engine invokes at spawn to bridge the typed entry to the type-erased boundary.
acceptance: workflow/define.gleam defines an opaque type WorkflowDefinition(input, output, error) and define(name: String, input_codec: Codec(input), output_codec: Codec(output), error_codec: Codec(error), entry_fn: fn(input) -> Result(output, error)) -> WorkflowDefinition(input, output, error).

A doc comment states the AF/AE/AP contract precisely: the engine (AE) consumes the WorkflowDefinition at spawn — it calls the package's entry function (named in the manifest), receives this value, and uses the codecs to decode the input Payload and encode the result/error Payload; aion-package (AP) records only the entry module and function NAMES (strings) in the manifest and does not introspect this Gleam value.

The WorkflowDefinition carries enough to bridge the typed entry to the type-erased ffi boundary (decode input on entry via input_codec, encode output via output_codec / error via error_codec on completion); the SDK does not invoke entry_fn itself (AE spawns the process and drives it).
A test constructs a WorkflowDefinition for a concrete typed workflow function and asserts the name and the three codecs are carried and recoverable.

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement the engine-side activity dispatch, recording, replay, or determinism context — those are AE/AD. This brief binds to them via ffi.
SHALL NOT implement timers, signals, queries, children, or concurrency — those are AF-006 through AF-010.
SHALL NOT build the .aion archive — that is AP; this brief provides the typed entry contract it consumes.
verification: gleam test passes for run, determinism, and define tests.
No wall-clock or entropy binding is exposed; no side_effect escape hatch; no panic/todo in new code.

checklist.text: workflow.run(Activity(i, o)) -> Result(o, ActivityError) is the single recorded activity dispatch, binding to the engine via ffi and decoding the typed result.
workflow.now() -> Timestamp binds to AD's determinism context (recorded event timestamp); the SDK exposes no wall-clock binding.
workflow.random() -> Float and workflow.random_int bind to AD's seeded RNG; the SDK exposes no entropy-source binding.
There is no generic side_effect(fn) escape hatch; the only side-effect dispatch is workflow.run (and all/race/map over activities).
workflow.define(name, input_codec, output_codec, error_codec, entry_fn) registers a typed entry contract consumable by the .aion manifest (AP).

stories.text: As a workflow author, I want a single recorded way to invoke a side effect (workflow.run over an activity) and plain Gleam everywhere else, so that the recorded/deterministic boundary is obvious from the code's shape.

As a workflow author, I want workflow.now and workflow.random to be deterministic and to have no wall-clock alternative available, so that my workflow cannot accidentally become non-deterministic and desynchronise replay.

As the engine, I want user data to arrive as an encoded payload with a typed codec on the SDK side so that my type-erased event store and replay never need to know the workflow's concrete Gleam types.

constraints.text: Pure Gleam package. No Rust, no engine code, no beamr dependency inside aion_flow. It publishes to Hex standalone (gleam_stdlib is its only runtime dependency, plus gleam_json for the default JSON codecs).
Every @external(erlang, ...) declaration lives in the single internal module aion/internal/ffi. No author-facing module declares an @external (per D1).

Module files contain logic; the package root and any mod-equivalent re-export aggregator stay declarations/re-exports only. No god files — split before a module exceeds ~500 lines of code (excluding comments/whitespace).

No partial implementations. Every public function is complete and total: no panic, no todo, no assert-as-control-flow; failure paths return typed Result errors. Gleam has no exceptions for control flow — every fallible binding returns Result.

Type safety is enforced, not advisory: activity input/output, signal payload, query return, and child input/output types are static type parameters; an invalid composition fails `gleam build`. A type-safety test brief asserts representative invalid compositions are rejected (compile-fail expectations documented).

Determinism: the SDK exposes workflow.now/workflow.random bound to AD's determinism context and exposes NO binding to a wall clock or entropy source. Workflow-code documentation states the prohibition explicitly.

The recorded/side-effectful boundary is structural: the only side-effect dispatch the SDK offers is workflow.run(activity) (and all/race/map over activities). There is no generic side_effect(fn) escape hatch (per D4).

No hardcoded defaults for configurable values: no default RetryPolicy, no default timeout, no default backoff numbers baked into the SDK. An activity without a retry decorator runs once; the author supplies all policy values (CLAUDE.md no-assumed-defaults).
User data crosses the boundary only through a Codec(a); a decode failure is a typed DecodeError, never a panic. No primitive silently swallows a decode error.

aion_flow does not implement engine mechanics (timer firing, signal routing, replay) — those are AE/AD/AT, reached via @external. It does not author NIFs in Rust — that is AN. It is not packaged into .aion — that is AP. The cluster owns only the Gleam API surface and its binding declarations.
All acceptance uses Gleam tooling: gleam build, gleam check, gleam format --check, gleam test must all pass clean. The test harness (aion/testing) runs under gleam test with no external services.

resolved_context.intention: Define aion_flow, the Gleam package (published to Hex) that workflow authors import to write durable workflows. It is the human-facing surface of the entire engine: a workflow author writes a plain, statically-typed Gleam function — decisions, loops, data transformation — and reaches the outside world only through this SDK's activity, signal, query, timer, and child-workflow primitives. The package is pure Gleam with no engine code inside it; every primitive is a thin @external(erlang) binding that resolves at runtime, when beamr loads the compiled workflow and the engine has registered the matching NIFs (AE-004), to the engine-side mechanics in AE/AD/AT. The defining property is compile-time type safety: activity inputs and outputs, signal payloads, and query returns are all statically typed, so an invalid composition — passing a String where an activity expects an Order, awaiting a signal of the wrong type, returning the wrong type from a query handler — fails at `gleam build`, not three days into a long-running production workflow. This is the concrete advantage over Temporal's SDKs (Go weakly typed, TypeScript better but runtime-checked). The package also ships aion/testing, the Temporal-equivalent test harness: simulate time so a month-long sleep resolves instantly, mock activities to return canned results, and assert that a workflow replays to the same state — all in plain gleam test, with no engine, no beamr, and no store.
